content_en:
The Hijra — the migration from Makkah to Madinah in 622 CE — is among the most pivotal events in Islamic history. Its significance was recognized by the early Muslim community: the Islamic calendar begins not with the birth of the Prophet, not with the first revelation, but with the Hijra, reflecting a communal judgment that this event marked the beginning of the Islamic civilization as a social and political reality. Mubarakpuri's account is among the most detailed and evocative treatments of this dramatic episode.

The background to the Hijra was the growing opposition in Makkah and the extraordinary development in Yathrib (later Madinah). At the pilgrimage season, twelve men from Yathrib had pledged their allegiance to the Prophet in the First Pledge of Aqabah, followed the next year by seventy-three men and two women in the Second Pledge. These Madinans — the Ansar (Helpers) — promised to protect the Prophet as they would protect their own families, providing a secure base for the migration.

When the Quraysh's conspiracy to assassinate the Prophet was revealed through divine warning, the Prophet instructed his Companions to begin migrating to Madinah. The departure of the Prophet himself, accompanied by Abu Bakr, was a scene of extraordinary drama: slipping out of Makkah at night while Ali slept in his bed as a decoy, the Prophet and Abu Bakr took an unusual southern route toward a cave in Mount Thawr where they hid for three days. The cave episode — when the Quraysh search party came within feet of their hiding place and Abu Bakr's anxiety prompted the divine consolation 'Do not grieve; indeed Allah is with us' (9:40) — is one of the most intimate moments of prophetic biography.

Mubarakpuri traces the journey from Thawr to Madinah through the desert, guided by the tracker Abdullah ibn Urayqit, passing through places that later generations would identify and honor. The reception at Quba — where the Prophet rested for days and laid the foundation of the first mosque in Islamic history — and the triumphal entry into Yathrib/Madinah are described with reference to the contemporary sources: the women and children of the Ansar singing songs of welcome, the crowd pressing to hold the bridle of the Prophet's camel, and the Prophet choosing the site of his mosque by allowing the camel to stop where it would.

The chapter also covers the remarkable institution-building that immediately followed the arrival in Madinah: the construction of the Prophet's mosque (which served simultaneously as worship space, community meeting place, and administrative center), the establishment of the brotherhood (mu'akhat) between the Muhajirun and Ansar, and the drafting of the Constitution of Madinah — a remarkable political document that organized the diverse tribes and communities of Madinah into a single polity under the Prophet's leadership. This immediate constitutional ordering reflects the Prophet's extraordinary capacity for practical governance alongside his spiritual mission.
content_id:
Hijrah — migrasi dari Makkah ke Madinah pada tahun 622 Masehi — termasuk di antara peristiwa-peristiwa paling penting dalam sejarah Islam. Signifikansinya diakui oleh komunitas Muslim awal: kalender Islam tidak dimulai dengan kelahiran Nabi ﷺ, bukan dengan wahyu pertama, melainkan dengan Hijrah, mencerminkan penilaian komunal bahwa peristiwa ini menandai dimulainya peradaban Islam sebagai kenyataan sosial dan politik. Kisah Mubarakpuri adalah salah satu uraian paling rinci dan berkesan tentang episode dramatis ini.

Latar belakang Hijrah adalah meningkatnya oposisi di Makkah dan perkembangan luar biasa di Yatsrib (kemudian Madinah). Pada musim haji, dua belas orang dari Yatsrib telah membaiat Nabi ﷺ dalam Baiat Aqabah Pertama, diikuti tahun berikutnya oleh tujuh puluh tiga orang laki-laki dan dua orang perempuan dalam Baiat Kedua. Orang-orang Madinah ini — kaum Anshar (Para Penolong) — berjanji untuk melindungi Nabi ﷺ sebagaimana mereka melindungi keluarga mereka sendiri, menyediakan basis yang aman untuk hijrah.

Ketika konspirasi Quraisy untuk membunuh Nabi ﷺ terungkap melalui peringatan ilahi, Nabi ﷺ memerintahkan para Sahabatnya untuk mulai berhijrah ke Madinah. Keberangkatan Nabi ﷺ sendiri, ditemani Abu Bakr, adalah sebuah adegan yang luar biasa dramatis: menyelinap keluar dari Makkah di malam hari sementara Ali tidur di tempat tidurnya sebagai pengalih perhatian, Nabi ﷺ dan Abu Bakr mengambil jalur selatan yang tidak biasa menuju sebuah gua di Gunung Tsur di mana mereka bersembunyi selama tiga hari. Episode gua — ketika rombongan pencari Quraisy datang hanya beberapa langkah dari tempat persembunyian mereka dan kegelisahan Abu Bakr memunculkan penghiburan ilahi 'Janganlah engkau bersedih; sesungguhnya Allah bersama kita' (9:40) — adalah salah satu momen paling intim dalam biografi kenabian.

Mubarakpuri menelusuri perjalanan dari Tsur ke Madinah melewati gurun, dipandu oleh penunjuk jalan Abdullah bin Urayqit, melewati tempat-tempat yang kemudian akan dikenali dan dihormati oleh generasi-generasi berikutnya. Penyambutan di Quba — di mana Nabi ﷺ beristirahat beberapa hari dan meletakkan fondasi masjid pertama dalam sejarah Islam — dan masuknya yang gemilang ke Yatsrib/Madinah digambarkan dengan mengacu pada sumber-sumber sezaman: perempuan-perempuan dan anak-anak Anshar menyanyikan lagu-lagu penyambutan, kerumunan yang berebut untuk memegang kekang unta Nabi ﷺ, dan Nabi ﷺ memilih lokasi masjidnya dengan membiarkan unta berhenti di mana pun dia mau.

Bab ini juga mencakup pembangunan kelembagaan yang luar biasa yang segera mengikuti kedatangan di Madinah: pembangunan masjid Nabi ﷺ (yang berfungsi sekaligus sebagai tempat ibadah, tempat pertemuan komunitas, dan pusat administratif), pembentukan persaudaraan (mu'akhat) antara kaum Muhajirin dan Anshar, serta penyusunan Piagam Madinah — sebuah dokumen politik yang luar biasa yang mengorganisasi berbagai suku dan komunitas Madinah menjadi satu kesatuan politik di bawah kepemimpinan Nabi ﷺ. Pengaturan konstitusional yang segera ini mencerminkan kemampuan Nabi ﷺ yang luar biasa dalam tata kelola praktis di samping misi spiritualnya.
content_ar: The Hijra — the migration from مكة المكرمة to المدينة المنورة in 622 CE — is among the most pivotal events in Islamic history. Its significance was recognized by the early مسلم الجماعة: the Islamic calendar begins not with the birth of النبي ﷺ, not with the أولاً الوحي, but with the Hijra, reflecting a communal judgment that this event marked the beginning of the Islamic civilization as a social and political reality. Mubarakpuri's account is among the most detailed and evocative treatments of this dramatic episode. The background to the Hijra was the growing opposition in مكة المكرمة and the extraordinary development in Yathrib (later المدينة المنورة). At the pilgrimage season, twelve men from Yathrib had pledged their allegiance to النبي ﷺ in the أولاً Pledge of Aqabah, followed the next year by seventy-three men and two women in the ثانياً Pledge. These Madinans — the Ansar (Helpers) — promised to protect النبي ﷺ as they would protect their own families, providing a secure base for the migration. عندما the Quraysh's conspiracy to assassinate النبي ﷺ was revealed through divine warning, النبي ﷺ instructed his الصحابة to begin migrating to المدينة المنورة. The departure of النبي ﷺ himself, accompanied by أبو بكر, was a scene of extraordinary drama: slipping out of مكة المكرمة at night بينما علي slept in his bed as a decoy, النبي ﷺ and أبو بكر took an unusual southern route toward a cave in Mount Thawr where they hid for three days. The cave episode — عندما the Quraysh search party came within feet of their hiding place and أبو بكر's القلق prompted the divine consolation 'Do not grieve; indeed الله is with us' (9:40) — is one of the most intimate moments of prophetic biography. Mubarakpuri traces the journey from Thawr to المدينة المنورة through the desert, guided by the tracker Abdullah ibn Urayqit, passing through places that later generations would identify and الشرف. The reception at Quba — where النبي ﷺ rested for days and laid the foundation of the أولاً المسجد in Islamic history — and the triumphal entry into Yathrib/المدينة المنورة are described with reference to the contemporary sources: the women and الأولاد of the Ansar singing songs of welcome, the crowd pressing to hold the bridle of النبي ﷺ's camel, and النبي ﷺ choosing the site of his المسجد by allowing the camel to stop where it would. The الباب أيضاً covers the remarkable institution-building that immediately followed the arrival in المدينة المنورة: the construction of النبي ﷺ's المسجد (which served simultaneously as worship space, الجماعة meeting place, and administrative center), the establishment of the brotherhood (mu'akhat) between the Muhajirun and Ansar, and the drafting of the Constitution of المدينة المنورة — a remarkable political document that organized the diverse tribes and communities of المدينة المنورة into a single polity under النبي ﷺ's leadership.
